school: House of Wisdom, Baghdad — First to systematically introduce Greek philosophy into the Islamic world

books.title: Fi al-Falsafa al-Ula (On First Philosophy)
books.note: First systematic philosophical treatise in Arabic — the founding document of Islamic philosophy.

books.title: Risala fi Hudud al-Ashya (On Definitions of Things)
Al-Madina al-Fadila (The Virtuous City)
books.note: Islamic political philosophy's founding text — the ideal Islamic city-state modelled on Plato's Republic.

books.title: Kitab al-Musiqa al-Kabir (The Great Book of Music)

school: Andalusian mystical philosophy — first major philosopher of Andalusia; influenced Ibn Arabi and Asín Palacios

books.title: Kitab al-Tabsira
books.url: ,note:"Mystical philosophy — first systematic philosophical treatise written in al-Andalus

school: Islamic Peripatetic / Avicennian Philosophy — crossover with Sufism via Visionary Recitals

books.title: Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb (The Canon of Medicine)
books.note: Medical encyclopedia used in European universities until the 17th century — the most influential medical text ever written.

books.title: Kitab al-Shifa (The Book of Healing) — 18 vols
Hayy ibn Yaqzan (Living Son of the Awake — Mystical Recital)
books.note: His mystical writing — influenced Sufis and later Islamic philosophers.

school: Ismaili Philosophy / Fatimid Cairo — Converted to Ismaili Islam after a dream; became chief Ismaili missionary in Khorasan

books.title: Safarnama (Book of Travels)
books.note: Most important Persian travel memoir of the medieval period — describes Fatimid Cairo and Jerusalem.

school: Andalusian Philosophy — Introduced Ibn Rushd to the caliph; his novel shaped European philosophy

books.title: Hayy ibn Yaqzan (Alive, Son of Awake)
books.note: First philosophical novel in world literature — a child raised alone on a desert island discovers God through reason alone. Influenced Locke and Defoe's Robinson Crusoe.

school: Islamic Peripatetic Philosophy — Aristotelian School; in direct debate with al-Ghazali

books.title: Tahafut al-Tahafut (The Incoherence of the Incoherence)
books.note: Systematic refutation of al-Ghazali's attack on philosophy; the greatest medieval philosophical debate.

books.title: Tahafut al-Tahafut (English)
books.note: Incoherence of the Incoherence — defence of philosophy against Al-Ghazali

school: Ishraqiyya (Illuminationist) School — Founder. Executed by Saladin's court 1191.

books.title: Hikmat al-Ishraq (Philosophy of Illumination)
books.note: Founded the Illuminationist school of Islamic philosophy.

school: Akbarian School / Wahdat al-Wujud (Unity of Being) — Founder

books.title: Fusus al-Hikam (Bezels of Wisdom)
books.note: Summit of Islamic metaphysics. ~560 books written as commentaries on it.

school: Andalusian mystical philosophy — correspondent of Frederick II; controversial unitive mystic
Isfahan School — teacher of Mulla Sadra; pioneer of the school of Isfahan Illuminationist philosophy

books.title: Kitab al-Qabasat
books.url: ,note:"Major work on eternal createdness — his central philosophical concept

school: Muta'aliya (Transcendent) Philosophy — Synthesised Ibn Sina, Suhrawardi and Ibn Arabi into the most comprehensive Islamic philosophy

books.title: Al-Asfar al-Arba'a (The Four Journeys of the Intellect — 9 vols)
books.note: The magnum opus of Islamic philosophy — a complete philosophical system integrating reason, mysticism and revelation.

school: Islamic Modernism / Sufi-Influenced Philosophy — Synthesised Rumi, Nietzsche, Bergson and Islamic thought; his vision shaped Pakistani independence

books.title: Javid Nama (The Book of Eternity — Persian)
books.note: His masterwork — a cosmic journey modelled on Dante's Divine Comedy, guided by the spirit of Rumi.

books.title: Asrar-i Khudi (Secrets of the Self — Persian)

school: Shadhiliyya — French metaphysician who converted to Islam; founder of the Traditionalist school; moved to Cairo and lived as a Sufi

books.title: The Crisis of the Modern World
books.url: ,note:"Foundational critique of modernity from a metaphysical perspective

school: Algerian School of Islamic Reform — developed the Civilization Equation (Man + Soil + Time); coined 'colonizability' — the internal condition that allows a society to be dominated; influenced by Ibn Khaldun and Ben Badis

books.title: Les Conditions de la Renaissance (Conditions for Renaissance)

school: Traditionalist school — leading contemporary Islamic philosopher; student of Frithjof Schuon; taught at Harvard and GWU

books.title: Knowledge and the Sacred
books.url: ,note:"Major work on Islamic metaphysics and perennial philosophy
